articles.title: GitHub added `total_pull_requests_merged` by AI adoption phase to Copilot enterprise/org reports, so tea…
articles.summary: GitHub added `total_pull_requests_merged` by AI adoption phase to Copilot enterprise/org reports, so teams can measure absolute PR throughput by AI-usage cohort, not just per-user averages. https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-26-track-total-merges-by-adoption-phase-in-enterprise-and-organization-reports/ — official GitHub Changelog; materially new vs yesterday’s MAI-Code-1-Flash item.
articles.emailSummary: GitHub added `total_pull_requests_merged` by AI adoption phase to Copilot enterprise/org reports, so teams can measure absolute PR throughput by…
articles.whyItMatters: This is one of the vetted AI signals from Dustin’s Daily AI Briefing, split into its own card so the morning scan covers multiple AI developments instead of one giant article.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is an AI product or policy update. The useful read is not that AI is changing; it is exactly what capability, access, cost, or risk changed in this article. The teaching angle is practical impact: who can use it, what it changes in work, and what risk or limit would matter in a real business setting. The main terms to understand are AI capability, guardrail, model, vendor dependency, workflow, governance. Read for the concrete new fact, the affected users, whether this is a launch or restriction, and whether the details gives enough evidence to trust it.

glossary.definition: A specific thing an AI tool can now do, such as use a browser, run code, connect tools, or access a model.
glossary.whyItMatters: Reading AI news gets easier when you focus on the exact new capability instead of the hype around the product.

glossary.definition: A rule, limit, approval step, or monitoring system meant to keep AI behavior safe and controlled.
glossary.whyItMatters: Guardrails determine whether a tool is safe enough for real work, especially when it touches business systems.

glossary.definition: The AI system doing the predicting or writing, like Claude, GPT, or Gemini.
glossary.whyItMatters: Model choice affects cost, quality, speed, policy limits, and whether a workflow can keep running when access changes.

glossary.definition: Reliance on one company, model, or platform for an important workflow.
glossary.whyItMatters: If access, pricing, or policy changes, dependency can suddenly become an operational risk.

glossary.definition: A repeatable sequence of steps people or software use to get work done.
glossary.whyItMatters: Workflow impact is where AI changes become practical: what step gets faster, safer, cheaper, or more automated?

glossary.definition: Rules, approvals, monitoring, and controls that keep AI or data use safe and reliable.
glossary.whyItMatters: Governance is the difference between a cool demo and something a business can safely let touch real work.

articles.title: Hugging Face published Agentic Resource Discovery: a draft open spec for agents to search registries of…
articles.summary: Hugging Face published Agentic Resource Discovery: a draft open spec for agents to search registries of tools, skills, and other agents instead of pre-installing every MCP/tool integration. https://huggingface.co/blog/agentic-resource-discovery-launch — official HF blog; practical plumbing, not model hype.

articles.whyItMatters: This is one of the vetted AI signals from Dustin’s Daily AI Briefing, split into its own card so the morning scan covers multiple AI developments instead of one giant article.

glossary.definition: AI software that can plan and take steps toward a task instead of only answering one prompt.
Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.
The AI system doing the predicting or writing, like Claude, GPT, or Gemini.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is an AI-agent workflow story: AI is being used to help define, run, or supervise a sequence of work instead of only chatting. The teaching angle is control. Once AI can trigger workflows, the key concepts are permissions, sandboxing, auditability, and whether humans can understand what it changed. The main terms to understand are agentic workflow, MCP, model, agentic, AI capability, guardrail. Read for the exact job the agent can do, what tools it touches, what permissions it starts with, and what safety controls are named in the story.

glossary.definition: A workflow where AI is expected to plan steps, use tools, and move work forward instead of only answering a single question.
glossary.whyItMatters: This is where AI starts touching real operations, so permissions, logs, and safety controls matter as much as the model output.

glossary.definition: Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.
glossary.whyItMatters: MCP matters because it is becoming a common plug shape for connecting AI agents to real tools, files, and services.

glossary.definition: The AI system doing the predicting or writing, like Claude, GPT, or Gemini.
glossary.whyItMatters: Model choice affects cost, quality, speed, policy limits, and whether a workflow can keep running when access changes.

glossary.definition: AI software that can plan and take steps toward a task instead of only answering one prompt.
glossary.whyItMatters: This term appears in the article, so understanding it helps you read the source without getting lost in jargon.

glossary.definition: The specific new thing an AI system can do, such as use a browser, run code, connect tools, or access a model.
glossary.whyItMatters: AI news is easier to judge when you identify the exact capability instead of treating every update as general hype.

glossary.definition: A rule, limit, approval step, or monitoring system meant to keep AI behavior safe and controlled.
glossary.whyItMatters: Guardrails determine whether a tool is safe enough for real work, especially when it touches business systems.

articles.title: Hugging Face also published a concrete pattern for testing whether software is “agentic enough”: measure…
articles.summary: Hugging Face also published a concrete pattern for testing whether software is “agentic enough”: measure the whole agent run against real tooling, not just final answer correctness. https://huggingface.co/blog/is-it-agentic-enough — official HF blog; useful eval pattern for Hermes/Data Nerve.

articles.title: PPL Corporation earnings outlook and clean energy strategy, shares tracked against utilities peers
articles.summary: Ad-hoc-news.de is reporting on PPL Corporation earnings outlook and clean energy strategy, shares tracked against utilities peers. In plain English, this is a customer-bill and rate-case story: the important details are what regulators approved, how much bills may change, what the utility says the money funds, and what protections or affordability terms are included. For Dustin, the useful read is whether this changes the local utility story around capacity, reliability, rates, regulation, metering, or the data products leaders need to explain those tradeoffs clearly. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
articles.emailSummary: A utility story tied to LG&E/KU/PPL that may affect rates, reliability, regulation, or planning.
articles.whyItMatters: This is close to Dustin’s utility analytics world: it may affect metering, reliability, customer impact, rate context, regulatory reporting, or the data products leaders need to trust.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is a utility-pricing story: a company, regulator, or market signal is pointing to costs that may eventually show up in customer bills. The teaching angle is the bridge between utility spending and public impact. Technical investments become controversial when customers are asked to pay for them. The main terms to understand are utility rates, ratepayer, data center, large load, grid upgrades, regulatory review. Read for the requested amount, the approved amount, the reason for the cost, and whether the story describes customer protections or opposition.

glossary.definition: The prices customers pay for electric, gas, or water service, usually set or reviewed by regulators.
glossary.whyItMatters: Rates are where utility planning, infrastructure costs, and company finances turn into customer impact.

glossary.definition: A utility customer who pays electric, gas, or water bills.
glossary.whyItMatters: Ratepayer impact is the consumer angle: whether ordinary customers end up paying for a utility decision.

glossary.definition: A large building full of servers. For utilities, it can mean a big new electricity customer that may need grid upgrades.
glossary.whyItMatters: Data centers can consume huge amounts of electricity, so they can change utility forecasts, infrastructure plans, and customer-bill debates.

glossary.definition: A customer or facility that uses enough electricity to affect utility planning, grid equipment, or rates.
glossary.whyItMatters: Large loads matter because one customer can be big enough to change planning assumptions for the whole grid area.

glossary.definition: Physical or operational improvements to the electric system so it can carry and deliver enough power safely.
glossary.whyItMatters: The key question is who benefits from the upgrade and who pays for it.

glossary.definition: The process where regulators examine utility plans, costs, reliability claims, and customer impacts.
glossary.whyItMatters: Regulatory review is where private utility decisions become public questions about fairness, reliability, and bills.
